eco_baseline_footprint.formula: BIF = (vCPU x 15 EUR) + (Storage GB x 0.08 EUR)
eco_baseline_footprint.method_note: RAM is considered included in compute cost and is not monetized separately in the operationalized framework model. The selected MCC represents a validator-class node participating in the current validator-based IOTA network and is used exclusively as a normalized comparative baseline rather than as a full validator committee deployment.
eco_baseline_footprint.source: Framework cost model applied to the IOTA validator-class MCC; IOTA validator node documentation

ps_throughput.estimation_note: Benchmark-level throughput value used as a technology-level classification input for the current validator-based IOTA architecture. It reflects documented high-throughput protocol performance rather than throughput measured on the normalized MCC.

eco_variable_transaction_cost.estimation_note: Approximate low protocol-level transaction fee under the validator-based IOTA gas-pricing model, used as a representative benchmark value for the current architecture. The value is strictly greater than zero because the current validator-based IOTA protocol is not feeless.
eco_variable_transaction_cost.source: IOTA gas pricing documentation and supporting fee examples

eco_storage_footprint.source: Framework baseline storage assumption for the IOTA validator-class MCC

parallel_execution.motivation: IOTA supports parallel validation and processing of transactions across an object-centric/DAG-based ledger structure rather than forcing all activity into a single strictly sequential blockchain.

horizontal_partitioning.motivation: The object-centric model and validator-committee architecture structurally distribute transaction processing and state across protocol objects rather than relying on one monolithic sequential execution path.

absence_of_global_serialization.motivation: IOTA does not rely on a single globally serialized longest-chain process in the sense of linear blockchain architectures; transaction certification is coordinated through the validator-committee and object-based model rather than through one universal sequential chain.

scalable_coordination_model.motivation: Despite these scalability-oriented design choices, write coordination still depends on validator-committee consensus and checkpoint/certification mechanisms, which remain a structural coordination layer rather than eliminating coordination bottlenecks altogether.

ps_scalability.source: IOTA validator-based architecture and consensus model

append_only_structural_history.motivation: Committed transactions and certified checkpoints form an append-only ledger history under the native protocol model.

cryptographic_linkage_between_records.motivation: Transactions, objects, and certified checkpoints are cryptographically linked through hashes and authenticated protocol references.

cryptographic_write_authentication.motivation: Transactions are authenticated through digital signatures and validator certification.

independent_integrity_verification.motivation: Certificates, signatures, object-state transitions, and finalized checkpoints can be independently verified under protocol rules.

rci_tamper_evidence_auditability.source: IOTA object model, transaction lifecycle, and validator committee model

protocol_enforced_irreversible_commit.motivation: Commit is based on validator-certified transactions and checkpoint finalization under the native committee protocol.

multi_party_confirmation_requirement.motivation: Transaction certification and finality require quorum participation by multiple validators.

absence_of_unilateral_rollback_capabilit.motivation: No single validator can unilaterally revoke a certified or finalized transaction under the native protocol rules.

rci_finality_rollback_resistance.source: IOTA validator committee and transaction lifecycle documentation

rci_write_availability_fault_tolerance.configuration_note: Technology-level score based on the native validator committee model rather than on the normalized single-node MCC abstraction.

absence_of_single_writer_requirement.motivation: Transaction certification and ledger progression are distributed across the validator committee rather than delegated to a single privileged writer.

quorum_based_write_continuation.motivation: The protocol can continue certifying and finalizing transactions as long as the validator committee preserves the required quorum threshold.

automatic_write_continuation_mechanism.motivation: Ordinary write progression does not require manual failover from a single validator because the protocol is designed for committee-based continuation under tolerated validator failures.

multi_node_write_survivability.motivation: Failure of an individual validator does not structurally disable the write path provided that the committee quorum remains available.

rci_write_availability_fault_tolerance.source: IOTA validator committee and consensus model

absence_of_single_entity_unilateral_stat.motivation: State validation and commit depend on the validator committee rather than on a single operator.

structural_requirement_for_multi_entity_.motivation: Transaction certification and finality require quorum participation by multiple validators.

validator_admission_independence.motivation: Validator participation is governed by protocol-level staking and committee rules rather than by a fixed enterprise permissioning authority.

absence_of_structurally_fixed_validator_.motivation: The validator set is epoch-based and can change at reconfiguration boundaries.

ags_control_distribution_governance.source: IOTA governance and validator committee model

native_privilege_differentiation_capabil.motivation: The object ownership model and cryptographic keys do not constitute a native enterprise identity and privilege management framework.

native_identity_lifecycle_governance_cap.motivation: The protocol does not provide enterprise-style native identity lifecycle governance comparable to permissioned DLT membership services.

structural_independence_of_identity_gove.motivation: The protocol does not include a native identity and access governance layer in the sense required by this criterion.

non_unilateral_compromise_requirement.motivation: A single validator cannot unilaterally compromise transaction certification or finality under the committee protocol.

majority_compromise_required.motivation: Compromising consensus outcomes requires coordinated control over a majority-scale fraction of validator voting power and, for safety-critical attacks, over the quorum threshold enforced by the protocol.

supermajority_compromise_required.motivation: Violating the BFT-style safety assumptions of the protocol requires control over a supermajority of validator voting power.

ags_collusion_resilience.source: IOTA validator committee and BFT quorum model
